title: It’s OK That You’re Not OK

chapters.title: The experience of loss and grief is a testimony to the bond of love and friendship that we have enjoyed in life
chapters.text:
There’s no quick fix to the pain that comes from losing a loved one. What we can do is learn how to tend to it. Dealing with loss supplies us with a special type of wisdom that can only be gained through experiencing grief. If we can learn to surmount the societal aversion for feelings, we will acquire the much-needed wisdom that comes from experiencing love and loss.

Our society teaches us to put a bandaid on the grief we feel from loss, but we need to let it train us to become better. Megan Devine, a psychotherapist with a decade of practice under her belt, learned this lesson from the sudden loss of her husband. Despite being a professional therapist, she found that helping those who suffer from the loss of a loved one is entirely different from experiencing it yourself. She discovered that the conventional approaches to grief are faulty and decided to share the lessons from her loss.

As loss and love are intertwined, we must harness it to create a life that copes with our sorrow in a meaningful way.

Megan Devine’s husband drowned in 2009, three days before his 40th birthday. This event led her to discover the failure of her profession in dealing with loss. She found that the rest of the world dismisses, judges, and misunderstands grieving people. And they do it inadvertently. Megan Devine has been able to fashion out an effective way of interacting with grief and supporting one another as we grapple with it.

In this summary, you will discover how to rehumanize grief to see it as an integral part of life. Then you’ll discover the skills needed to make good use of the change that loss brings.

Grief is not a disease that requires treatment. So we need to learn the skills and have the compassion necessary to navigate this experience. To love better during tough times, we must first comprehend the reality of grief.

chapters.title: Companionship, not correction, is what a person experiencing grief needs
chapters.text:
Our culture is always trying to solve grief as if it were a problem. Words sent to comfort grieving persons appear to either deny or diminish the sense of loss. Thus, you’re driven into greater depths of loneliness.

There is nothing wrong with grief. It’s a natural extension of love. It’s a healthy and sane response to loss. ~ Megan Devine

While some losses can be transformed into something useful, there is a kind of pain that just is. Slogans and platitudes cannot undo it. Instead, they make it worse by denying the reality of the loss or minimizing the magnitude of what has just happened.

You can learn to live inside your loss and carry with grace what cannot be fixed, but it must start with truth-telling.

You need to understand the truth about our emotional illiteracy and cultural aversion to pain. Then, you will explore how grief alters your body and soul. Equally important is the careful choice of your support system. And finally, you will chart a way forward where love and loss can peacefully coexist.

We live in a world that believes anything uncomfortable is wrong. So, professional and personal support for grieving people seeks to get them out of grief as quickly as possible.

We need to switch to truth mode by admitting that some losses cannot be transformed into something useful, have no reason for happening, and do not have a silver lining. We should admit that loving each other suggests losing each other. That’s when we will begin to learn how to live with the loss of someone we have loved. We need to learn how to lose just as much as we have learned how to love instead of seeing loss as something terrible to be avoided at all costs.

Survival after a loss is not procedural. There are no stages of grief. Our lack of vocabulary for dealing with loss comes from our cultural roots. That’s why people often impose a different reality on those who grieve, which differs from their own experience of loss. Choosing not to let people invalidate your grief makes things different, even if it can’t make it right.

chapters.title: People who seek to comfort a grieving person use words that do the opposite of their intention
chapters.text:
Close friends and acquaintances want to provide support but lack the right words. So, it is better to be quiet than to try to speak words of comfort. Most of the comforting words are dismissive of the pain. Consequently, those trying to help feel frustrated by their unsuccessful attempt to provide comfort.

Comforters fail at providing solace because they see grief as a problem to be fixed instead of a natural response to loss.

Sharing stories of loss is a common way of offering support, but it may not always be effective, as each loss is as individual as the love that came before it. That your husband died too does not mean you can fully comprehend this loss. You don’t know how it feels, so stop saying you do. While sharing stories of loss can be helpful at the right time and place, they are not appropriate in the early days of grief. It only leads to grief competition to see whose pain is worse.

Every loss is valid and should be honored, but it is wrong to compare one form of grief with another.

Feeling better doesn’t come from comparison. Another wrong way to console a grieving person is by using words of comfort that diminish the pain they feel. Comments like, “She wouldn’t want you to be sad” have an implied second half that makes the statements horribly wrong. The second half is, “So stop feeling bad.” Thus the statement is taken as “She wouldn’t want you to be sad (so stop feeling bad).”

Some words of comfort also suggest that the loss was necessary and has a greater purpose. Whatever lesson loss has to teach could be learned through many different means.

You can choose to build wholeness on top of your grief. That’s a choice you make, but you didn’t need the loss to make that choice. Saying you needed the loss to grow is an indication that you were powerless or guilty of something before the loss. That’s why words of comfort feel so bad that you begin to second-guess yourself.

chapters.title: Our culture’s grief illiteracy makes the already painful period worse
chapters.text:
Therapists have been trained to see grief in pathological terms. It is seen as a disorder—a response to a difficult situation that shouldn’t be allowed to linger. People see happiness as healthy and sorrow as unhealthy. Thus, they construct words that are intended to get you to your happy place. Sadly, these words have an impact that’s opposite to their intention.

Doctors classify persistent grieving that goes beyond six months as a disorder.

Therapists diagnose and treat grief wrongly because they expect patients to follow the five stages of grief model by Dr. Elisabeth Kübler-Ross. She published this model in 1969 in her book, On Death and Dying. Eventually, Kübler-Ross decried the misapplication of her model as a linear and universal procedure for dealing with grief. Her goal was to validate the experience of grief and not to linearize it. There are no stages of grief; there are just manifestations of grief that vary from one individual to another.

There are other reasons for the development of a grief illiterate culture besides solution-oriented messages and the trickle-down effect of the stages of grief model. The entertainment industry plays a subtle but significant role in the portrayal of grief in movies and music. They tell stories of redemption and the transformation of grief into something “useful.” Somehow, the hero is supposed to be happy again after suffering huge losses. We believe that we are meant to overcome, so we fetishize the struggle to celebrate the happy ending. Brené Brown, a social scientist and author of Rising Strong, says we live in “a Gilded Age of Failure.”

The effect of this ideology on grieving people is that they are considered abnormal when they don’t enact the storyline that says adversity is meant to be overcome. There is a need for a new cultural narrative that reflects the truth that unredeemable grief exists in the world, and we are to learn to carry it rather than surmount it.

chapters.title: Bearing witness to your grief is the middle ground between being eternally broken and completely healed
chapters.text:
We’ve seen the cultural roots of grief avoidance and how they put a gag order on telling the truth about grief. Tending properly to grief requires working towards the midpoint between denial and doom. That midpoint is where we experience the full breadth of grief as an extension of the love that preceded it. To bear witness to your grief is to choose to embrace it as one of the mysterious aspects of love instead of trying to master it.

When you allow grief to exist, you will find beautiful ways to tend to it. Stop resisting the pain so that you can gain the freedom to love and reduce suffering. We can act with self-compassion and extend the same courtesy to others. We cannot change the reality of pain, but we can unfold the full expression of love when we include the loss of the object of love in our narrative.

We need to express how we feel without having to defend our grief when people judge us for clinging to it. It will be easier on family and friends when they know they no longer have the role of solving our grief. All they need to do is help us carry the pain.

It is an act of fierce self-love and tenacity to express your grief in a culture that is emotionally illiterate.

With the right tools, you can chart your way to your middle ground. You no longer have to pretend you’ve mastered the pain or turn your back on your world because you are in pain.

Grief can occur early or late. So many tools exist to deal with later grief, but very few address the time when your grief was so sharp. So it is okay to look for what works for you in the early days of grief.

During early grief, one of the things that disappears is language. You can’t find the words to describe what you feel. It’s okay to acknowledge what you can and cannot do at this time.

chapters.title: Acknowledgment of the loss is a powerful tool that helps you deal with the new reality
chapters.text:
You can have a sense of normalcy during this abnormal time by admitting that this loss has happened. There is usually so much to do at this time—organizing memorials, planning the burial or cremation, writing eulogies, and responding to commitments that can’t wait. So, be willing to delegate because others can help. You can handle whatever feels intimate and important to you on your own.

Telling the story of your loss, whether verbally or through journaling, helps bring order to your mind during chaotic times.

Everywhere you go, there are land mines that remind you of the loss. So, allow yourself to take a break from interacting with people for as long as you need. Sometimes, it may be necessary to change your environment for a while. Practice self-kindness. Pace yourself and do not follow any timeline to return to business as usual.

Apply the vomit metric. When the thought of doing something like donating your child’s clothes, removing the wedding ring, etc., makes you feel sick or break out in hives, then it is not the right time.

Everyone grieves differently, and everyone has a different way they memorialize or acknowledge the person who is gone. ~ Megan Devine

You’re not obligated to remain at a memorial, even if you organized it or if it starts to make you feel uncomfortable. Respect other people’s grief as well. They may decline invitations to memorials or choose to grieve differently. Kids approach grief differently than adults. To assist our kids, we need to discuss pain, death, grief, and love in age-appropriate ways.

Family dynamics can become fractured. It is okay to be angry. But see emotions as a response to events. Repressing anger creates pressure that leads to toxic behaviors. Instead, have an ally who is willing to hear your anger. It could be a therapist or a trusted friend.

Did you know? When breathing, making your exhale longer than your inhale can soothe your fight-or-flight response.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Love is the way to reduce suffering. Embrace pain without being enveloped by it by connecting it with a heart that loves. Separating suffering from pain provides healing and support in grief. Suffering comes when your pain is dismissed or judged. Pain is a healthy response to the loss of someone you love.

When pain is unable to find expression, it creates an emotional backlog that creates more problems. Hence, instead of trying to correct or contain it, we need to tend to it. In grief, look for things that bring relief. Consider it an experiment to find what works for you. Keep a record of stressors and the things that bring relative calm. Things like self-compassion, the ability to respond to other people’s poor behavior, validation, and feeling rested are signs of wellness.

If you’re part of a support system for a grieving person, avoid taking the grief away from them through unsolicited suggestions and advice. Allow them to tend to the pain instead of referring to the past or talking about the future. State the truth: “This hurts. I love you. I’m here.”

Resist the temptation to try to fix the pain. Focus, instead, on how you can reduce the suffering. Remember, this is not about you, so develop the capacity to witness the pain. Instead of asking them to call you if they need you, tell them what you will do by making concrete offers. And keep your promises.

Ultimately, show love. This means you show up, say something, and do something. Listen more and educate others on what to do.

Try this

The truth about grief must combine with love to reduce suffering. If you’re in early grief, experiment with it to find things that work for you and things that stress you. Keep a record of your discovery and tend to the pain by focusing unapologetically on what works.

If you’re in later grief, remind yourself that you need an orientation, not mastery of the grief. Open yourself to love to honor the loss because loss is an extension of love.

If you’re providing support to someone grieving, watch your tongue. Resist the urge to diminish the grief or judge them.

Read it till the end?
